archetypes.name: The Scientist
archetypes.motivation: To prove or disprove. Knowledge is everything.

archetypes.name: The Veteran
archetypes.motivation: Seen war, survived. Hired for protection.
archetypes.vulnerability: What they've seen before doesn't prepare them for this

archetypes.name: The Journalist
archetypes.motivation: The story. Byline of a lifetime.
archetypes.skills: Asks questions others don't, records everything
archetypes.vulnerability: Will risk others for the scoop

archetypes.name: The Mechanic
archetypes.motivation: Keeps the planes flying, the radios working, the drills drilling.
archetypes.vulnerability: Rational mind that breaks when rationality fails

archetypes.name: The Patron's Agent
archetypes.motivation: Represents whoever's funding this. Has a hidden agenda.
archetypes.vulnerability: Loyalty divided between the group and the patron

archetypes.name: The Local Expert
archetypes.motivation: Whaler, sailor, guide. Knows the ice.
archetypes.vulnerability: Superstitious, or maybe just experienced enough to be afraid

archetypes.name: The Believer
archetypes.motivation: Actually read Dyer. Believes every word. Maybe obsessively.
archetypes.vulnerability: Already partially broken by what they believe is coming

npcs.name: Dr. Harold Starkweather
npcs.description: A former colleague of Dyer's who read the private account, not just the published one. Believes every word. Where Dyer concluded 'stay away,' Starkweather concluded 'go back prepared.' May be slightly unhinged by what he read.
npcs.agenda: Vindication — for Dyer, for science, for the truth. Included material in the briefing that Moore wanted cut. Has too much faith. Pushes the expedition forward when players hesitate.

npcs.name: Professor James Moore
npcs.role: Co-patron (Skeptic). Remains in the US, communicates by radio.
npcs.description: A hard-nosed empiricist brought on to keep the expedition credible. Thinks Dyer had a breakdown and that there's a rational explanation for everything. Added caveats to the briefing that Starkweather resents.
npcs.agenda: Debunking — or, if the evidence proves real, claiming the discovery for rigorous science. Keeps the expedition grounded via radio. His voice becomes increasingly distant, then silent as radio degrades.

npcs.name: The Ship Captain
npcs.description: Practical, skeptical, loyal to crew over mission.
npcs.agenda: Crew safety above all. Will resist orders that endanger the ship or its people.

npcs.name: The Radio Operator
npcs.role: The voice of the outside world
npcs.description: Maintains communication with the ship and Moore. When this voice goes silent, everything changes.
npcs.agenda: Keeping the link to civilization. Increasingly anxious as contact degrades.

npcs.name: The Dog Handler
npcs.description: The dogs are the early warning system. This person interprets their behavior — initially as weather sensitivity, later with growing unease.
npcs.agenda: Caring for the animals. The dogs know first; this person translates.

npcs.name: The Patron's Second Agent
npcs.description: There may be more than one person with a hidden agenda among the expedition members.
npcs.agenda: Secondary objectives unknown to the players. Reports to Moore or has independent instructions.

npcs.name: Echoes of the First Expedition
npcs.description: Not a living NPC. Journals, equipment, bodies, star-shaped graves. Dyer's team is present through what they left behind. Lake's private field journal with increasingly frantic entries.
npcs.agenda: The warning that went unheeded. Evidence that accumulates to destroy the 'camp disaster' story.

npcArchetypes.name: The Driven Leader
npcArchetypes.template: One-eyed Merchant Navy officer who pushed forward recklessly, prioritized urgency over safety. His commitment to the mission became indistinguishable from self-destruction. Walked onto thin ice against all advice and died.
npcArchetypes.useFor: NPC leaders whose duty overrides judgment. The dog handler pushing deeper. A mechanic insisting one more repair. Starkweather himself echoes this pattern.

npcArchetypes.name: The Pragmatist
npcArchetypes.template: Antarctic veteran, 'a strange mixture of fraud, flamboyance and ability.' Clashed with the leader over methods — he was right, but lacked authority. Effectively took command when things collapsed.
npcArchetypes.useFor: The experienced subordinate who sees disaster coming and can't stop it. The ship captain, a mechanic, anyone whose competence is overruled by rank.

npcArchetypes.name: The Witness
npcArchetypes.template: Youngest scientific member. Held the party together during the worst. Lived to 91. Left an inscription on the wall. Said the struggle was not futile — 'it was something the human spirit had accomplished.'
npcArchetypes.useFor: The one who survives and decides what it meant. A player character archetype, but also for NPCs who carry the story forward. The counterpoint to Lovecraftian nihilism.

npcArchetypes.name: The Volunteer Who Broke
npcArchetypes.template: Scottish priest, last-minute replacement. Had no idea what he was signing up for. Collapsed from exhaustion and scurvy on the return march. Died being carried on a sledge.
npcArchetypes.useFor: NPCs or player characters who are out of their depth. The civilian academic, the patron's secretary, anyone who joined for adventure and found something else.

npcArchetypes.name: The Adventurer
npcArchetypes.template: London finance clerk 'with a taste for adventure.' Recruited because he'd worked on a ranch. Survived the march, then walked out onto thin ice with Mackintosh and vanished.
npcArchetypes.useFor: Civilians who came for excitement. Their arc is the gap between what they expected and what they got.

npcArchetypes.name: The Steady Hand
npcArchetypes.template: Royal Navy petty officer. Did his duty everywhere. Concocted the tobacco substitute. Died of typhoid in Malta after the war — not the Antarctic, not the extraordinary, just the mundane.
npcArchetypes.useFor: The reliable NPC who keeps things running. The cook, the mechanic, the one who doesn't panic. Their death, if it comes, is the most affecting because they were the most dependable.

npcArchetypes.name: The Competent Man Without Credit
npcArchetypes.template: Kept the Aurora together through nine months of ice drift. Brought it to New Zealand. Was dismissed and replaced by a more senior officer for the rescue mission.
npcArchetypes.useFor: NPCs whose competence is unrewarded. The radio operator who keeps the link alive. The mechanic who saves the plane and gets no mention in the report.

npcArchetypes.name: The Kid
npcArchetypes.template: Recruited as a last-minute wireless operator. Survived. Went on to become a knight and industry chairman. Proof that surviving the extreme can forge a person.
npcArchetypes.useFor: Young NPCs or player characters. The graduate student, the assistant, anyone whose entire career begins with this expedition.

pool.name: Dr. Frank H. Pabodie
pool.tagline: The man who built the drill.
pool.portrait: Wire-rimmed glasses, calloused hands, graphite smudge on his temple. Moves with the deliberate economy of a man who has assembled heavy machinery in high winds.
pool.background: Professor of Engineering at Miskatonic University. Designed and built the experimental boring apparatus that made the 1930 expedition possible. When Starkweather proposed going back, Pabodie was the first man he called — not because Pabodie believed Dyer's account, but because no one else could keep the equipment running at forty below.
pool.motivation: The core samples from 1930 contained formations that shouldn't exist. Impossible things demand explanation, and explanation demands better equipment.
pool.fear: That the explanation will be something his instruments cannot measure and his training cannot accommodate.
relationships: Designed all drilling and boring equipment for both expeditions
Corresponded privately with Dyer before the old man stopped answering letters

pool.name: Dr. Amy Bryce
pool.tagline: She studies what lives where nothing should.
pool.portrait: Sharp jaw, dark hair pinned severely back, hands reddened from years of cold-water specimen work. Wears men's expedition boots because no one manufactures women's.
pool.background: Marine biologist from Newnham College, Cambridge. Published a controversial monograph on abyssal organisms that survive at pressures and temperatures previously considered incompatible with complex life. The first woman appointed to an Antarctic geological survey — a fact that required three letters to the Royal Geographical Society and a threat of resignation from Moore.
pool.motivation: Dyer's published report mentioned biological specimens that defied classification. She intends to classify them.
pool.fear: That the organisms she finds will be alive in ways that rewrite the definition of the word.
relationships: Recruited by Moore for her deep-sea organism research
Knows Pabodie by reputation — respects his rigour, suspects his imagination is limited

pool.name: Sgt. Douglas Rourke
pool.portrait: Weathered face, close-cropped grey hair, a scar along the left forearm he never explains. Stands like a man who learned to sleep standing up and never fully unlearned it.
pool.background: Served at Gallipoli with the ANZAC forces, then fourteen years as a private military contractor across three continents. Hired by Starkweather as expedition security after two other candidates read the briefing materials and declined. Rourke read them and named his price.
pool.motivation: The pay is extraordinary and the contract is simple: keep them alive until the ship comes back.
pool.fear: That he'll meet something in the ice that doesn't care how steady his hands are.
relationships: Hired directly by Starkweather, answers to no one else
Served briefly with a Norwegian unit — speaks enough Norse to trade insults with Sørensen

pool.name: Midge Caldwell
pool.tagline: The Arkham Gazette sent their cheapest reporter. She intends to make them regret it.
pool.portrait: Small, wiry, perpetually bundled in an oversized parka. Graflex Speed Graphic slung across her chest like a weapon. Pencil behind one ear, spare behind the other.
pool.background: Reporter for the Arkham Gazette. Got the Antarctic posting by being persistent, cheap, and willing to sign a liability waiver no sane person would accept. Covered the Innsmouth affair — or tried to, before the government sealed the records. Has a nose for the story behind the story.
pool.motivation: Something happened on Dyer's expedition that the university covered up. Front-page stories don't bury themselves.
pool.fear: That the story, when she finds it, will be one no editor will print and no reader will believe.
relationships: Assigned by the Gazette to produce weekly dispatches for the Sunday edition
Has already tried to interview Dyer — he refused, but his hands were shaking

pool.name: Thomas Gedney
pool.tagline: If it has moving parts, he can fix it. If it doesn't, he'll add some.
pool.portrait: Broad shoulders, oil-stained fingers that never quite come clean, a gap-toothed grin. Keeps a leather tool roll in his breast pocket the way other men keep cigarettes.
pool.background: Aircraft mechanic, grew up in his father's machine shop in Gardiner, Maine. Rebuilt his first engine at fourteen. Keeps the expedition's two Fairchild 71 monoplanes airworthy under conditions the manufacturer's manual politely describes as 'not recommended.' Has never read a book cover to cover but can diagnose a misfiring cylinder by ear at fifty yards.
pool.motivation: The machines need him. That's enough. It's always been enough.
pool.fear: That something will break that has no mechanism — no parts, no logic, nothing he can take apart and understand.
relationships: Works alongside Webb on electrical systems — they argue like brothers
Pabodie designed the drill; Gedney keeps it turning

pool.name: Elias Webb
pool.tagline: The expedition's link to the outside world. He hears things in the static others don't.
pool.portrait: Thin, angular, wire-rimmed spectacles. Fingers perpetually tapping as though keying Morse. A soldering iron burn on his right thumb that he considers a badge of honour.
pool.background: Radio operator and electrical technician. Builds crystal sets for recreation and has modified the expedition's Marconi equipment well beyond factory specifications. Grew up in a lighthouse on the Maine coast, where silence meant danger and the sound of static meant you were still connected to the living world.
pool.motivation: Maintain the link. As long as the radio works, they are not alone. As long as they are not alone, they can be saved.
pool.fear: That the signal he eventually receives will not come from any station he can identify.
relationships: Responsible for all communication between base camp, the ship, and Moore in the States
Gedney handles mechanical systems; Webb handles electrical — the division is sacred

pool.name: Reginald Price
pool.tagline: Starkweather's money man. Keeps two ledgers.
pool.portrait: Immaculate even at sixty below — pressed collar, polished shoes replaced with polished boots. Thin moustache, thinner smile. The leather-bound expense ledger is always visible. The second ledger, no one has seen.
pool.background: Starkweather's business manager for eleven years. Sent south to protect the patron's considerable investment and ensure the expedition produces results that justify the expense. Has no scientific training and no interest in acquiring any. Understands logistics, leverage, and the precise moment when an investment becomes a loss.
pool.motivation: Starkweather has staked his fortune and his reputation. Price is here to ensure at least one of those survives.
pool.fear: That what they find will be worth more than Starkweather can control — and that others will realize it first.
relationships: Reports to Starkweather but has authority over expedition finances and supply allocation
Moore distrusts him; the feeling is mutual and professional

pool.name: Dr. Catherine Ainsworth
pool.tagline: Moore's eyes and ears. Officially, a consulting geologist.
pool.portrait: Tall, composed, greying hair pulled into a practical bun. Wears a man's field jacket with the sleeves neatly altered. Carries a geological hammer and a codebook.
pool.background: Consulting geologist with a legitimate publication record and a less legitimate secondary role as Moore's scientific observer. Sent to keep the expedition honest — to ensure Starkweather's enthusiasm doesn't contaminate the data. Reports to Moore by coded radio on a frequency Price doesn't know about.
pool.motivation: The science must be clean. If what Dyer described is real, it must be documented with absolute rigour. If it isn't real, the expedition must say so.
pool.fear: That the evidence will be real and that rigour will not be enough to contain what it means.
relationships: Appointed by Moore as independent scientific oversight
Suspects Price is running a parallel intelligence operation for Starkweather

pool.name: Nils Sørensen
pool.tagline: Twenty years on Antarctic waters. The ice speaks to him.
pool.portrait: Broad, weatherbeaten face, salt-white beard, hands like knotted rope. Eyes permanently narrowed against glare. Smells of pipe tobacco and seal oil.
pool.background: Norwegian whaler, twenty years on Antarctic waters out of Sandefjord. Hired as ice pilot and survival consultant. Has overwintered twice on South Georgia and once — involuntarily — on the pack ice itself. Knows the ice the way a sailor knows the sea: by sound, by smell, by the way it groans at three in the morning.
pool.motivation: The expedition pays well and the ice is the only country he has left. He goes where the ice goes.
pool.fear: That the old sailors' stories about what lives under the deep ice are not stories.
relationships: Hired as ice pilot — his word on ice conditions overrides all other authority
Knew a man from Lars Larsen's whaling crew who spoke of stone ruins glimpsed from a crow's nest in 1929

pool.name: Jack McReady
pool.tagline: The ice doesn't lie to you the way people do.
pool.portrait: Lean, sun-darkened, frost-nipped ears. Moves with a sledge driver's rolling gait. Dog hair on every garment he owns. The dogs trust him before they trust anyone.
pool.background: Dog handler and sledge driver. Was with Byrd's first Antarctic expedition in '29, where he drove supply runs across the Ross Ice Shelf in conditions that killed two other teams. Came back to the ice because civilian life felt like wearing a suit that didn't fit. The dogs understand him. He understands the dogs. Everything else is negotiable.
pool.motivation: The dogs came back wrong from Dyer's expedition — the survivors were never right again. McReady wants to know what they saw.
pool.fear: That the dogs will refuse to go forward, and that he'll understand why.
relationships: Manages the expedition's dog teams and all sledge operations
Sørensen respects him; they communicate in grunts and gestures that convey more than most men's speeches

pool.name: Professor Malcolm Angell
pool.tagline: He read Dyer's private account. He correlated it with older texts. He knows what he expects to find.
pool.portrait: Hollow-cheeked, deep-set eyes, prematurely grey at forty-two. Ink stains on his cuffs. Carries a battered leather satchel containing three notebooks and a monograph he will never publish.
pool.background: Folklore scholar from Brown University. A distant cousin of the late Professor George Gammell Angell, whose papers on certain anomalous cults were suppressed after his death. Read Dyer's private account — the one Miskatonic locked in the restricted archive — after calling in favours that cost him two professional friendships. Has spent three years correlating Dyer's observations with Pnakotic fragments, Eltdown Shards, and marginalia in a water-damaged copy of the Necronomicon.
pool.motivation: Confirmation. The texts describe something. Dyer saw something. If they are the same something, then a pattern emerges — and patterns can be understood.
pool.fear: That he is right, and that understanding the pattern will not make it less terrible.
relationships: Secured his place on the expedition by providing Starkweather with translations Starkweather could not have obtained elsewhere
Bryce considers him a crank; he considers her narrow

pool.name: Sister Agnes Waverly
pool.tagline: She recognized Dyer's descriptions. She'd heard them before, in another tongue, on another shore.
pool.portrait: Weathered complexion, steady grey eyes, calloused hands from years of field nursing. Wears practical expedition clothing over a small silver cross she never removes. Moves with the unhurried competence of someone accustomed to emergencies.
pool.background: Former Church Missionary Society nurse. Served twelve years in remote stations across the Solomon Islands and New Guinea, treating tropical diseases and learning indigenous languages the Society preferred she didn't speak. Read Dyer's published account — the sanitized version — and recognized in his descriptions of alien geometry and impossible biology the same details she'd heard in oral traditions from three separate island cultures, separated by hundreds of miles of open ocean.
pool.motivation: The old stories are not superstition. They are warnings, preserved in the only medium that survives millennia — human memory. She is here to determine what they warned against.
pool.fear: That the warnings were not meant to prevent what is coming, but to prepare the survivors for what comes after.
relationships: Applied independently — Moore approved her as expedition medic; her real qualifications are ones he didn't ask about
Angell treats her as a fellow traveller; she treats him as a man who has read too much and listened too little
